feedback.text: I have recently become aware of the possibility of providing an opinion regarding the Digital Fairness Act. After reading the pivotal points, I generally agree with the above idea of protecting citizens from practices carried out by online companies, aimed exclusively at their own profit, making it difficult or in some cases impossible for citizens to defend themselves against them or find alternatives. While gambling in video games touches me more closely than the other points, I consider that, although it is a necessary measure, it is not sufficient for the complete protection of citizens (often minors talking about video games). I consider that another possible protection would be the safeguard of the digital product purchased (video games in this case) or at least the possibility of preserving it in some form that can be used. Paying for the use of a video game without having the slightest idea if and when the company selling it can remove it from each platform and prevents it from being used by the citizens who buy it is pure planned obsolescence. The company which creates and sells a video game by reserving the right to remove it and blocking its use to those who have bought it, have the power to decide when to do so by arbitrarily deciding on the end of the exploitation of that product, even though it is fully functional and operates in accordance with the sales contract established with the purchasing citizens. The Stop Destroying Videogames initiative seeks to prevent this from happening in the future, preventing video game producers from destroying their creations without leaving any trace and depriving citizens of a product they do not have a way to oppose. In my opinion, the Digital Fairness Act is perfectly in line with the principles of Stop Destroying Videogames, so it would be wise to fill in a sixth point of this Act by including the defence of Videogames and citizens who have lawfully bought a copy of it. I thank anyone reading this opinion and apologise for any mistakes and for the tortuositá of my writing. Matthew
